questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'for' attribute on a <label> element?
choices: It specifies the form the label belongs to
It associates the label with a specific form control by matching its id
It sets the label text direction
It defines the order of labels on the page
questions.explanation: The 'for' attribute on a <label> must match the 'id' attribute of the form control it labels. This creates an explicit association, so clicking the label focuses or activates the associated control.

questions.question: What is the difference between explicit and implicit label association?
choices: Explicit uses CSS, implicit uses JavaScript
Explicit uses the 'for' attribute, implicit wraps the control inside the <label> tag
Explicit is for checkboxes only, implicit is for text inputs only
There is no difference; they are the same thing
questions.explanation: Explicit association uses the 'for' attribute on the label matching the 'id' on the control. Implicit association wraps the form control inside the <label> element, requiring no 'for' or 'id' attributes.

questions.question: What happens when a user clicks on a <label> that is properly associated with a checkbox?
choices: The checkbox is toggled on or off
questions.explanation: When a label is properly associated with a checkbox (via 'for' attribute or wrapping), clicking the label toggles the checkbox. This increases the clickable area and improves usability, especially on mobile devices.

questions.question: Why are labels important for web accessibility?
choices: They make form controls visually larger
They allow screen readers to announce what each form control is for
They are required for form validation to work
They automatically translate form controls into other languages
questions.explanation: Labels are essential for accessibility because screen readers use the label text to announce what each form control is for. Without labels, users relying on assistive technology may not understand the purpose of form inputs.

questions.question: In explicit label association, the label's 'for' attribute must match the form control's ___ attribute.
questions.explanation: The 'for' attribute on a <label> creates an explicit association by referencing the 'id' attribute of the form control. For example: <label for="email"> pairs with <input id="email">.

questions.question: In ___ label association, the form control is placed inside the <label> element without needing a 'for' attribute.
questions.explanation: Implicit association is achieved by nesting the form control inside the <label> element. For example: <label>Email: <input type="email"></label>. No 'for' or 'id' attributes are needed.
